\subsection*{Workshop Chapter 1 (Python)}

In this workshop, you apply the probability-distribution concepts from Chapter 1 directly in Python.

\subsection*{Background on Python}

Python is widely used in data analysis because it combines readable syntax with a strong scientific ecosystem. In this notebook we use \ttblue{scipy.stats} to mirror the same probability calculations as the R workshop.

\subsection*{Integration with Theory}

Each exercise corresponds to a theory section in the chapter. Move back and forth between the chapter text and the code blocks to connect formulae, parameter meanings, and interpretation of results.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{import math}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{from scipy.stats import hypergeom, binom, poisson, norm, t, chi2, f}
\end{Verbatim}


\begin{exercise}
\textbf{Hypergeometric Distribution}


For the hypergeometric model, use \ttblue{hypergeom.pmf(k, N, M, n)} to calculate point probabilities $P(K = k)$.

To compute cumulative probabilities $P(K \textbackslash{}leq k)$, use \ttblue{hypergeom.cdf}. Right-tail probabilities $P(K > k)$ are obtained with \ttblue{hypergeom.sf} (the survival function).

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{N = 331}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{M = 17}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{n = 60}

\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{hypergeom.pmf(0, N, M, n), hypergeom.pmf(1, N, M, n)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{(0.03036497699701723, 0.12145990798806892)}
\end{Verbatim}

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{hypergeom.cdf(1, N, M, n), hypergeom.pmf(0, N, M, n) + hypergeom.pmf(1, N, M, n)}
\end{Verbatim}

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{hypergeom.sf(1, N, M, n)}
\end{Verbatim}


\end{exercise}

\begin{exercise}
\textbf{Binomial Distribution}


For the binomial approximation, use \ttblue{binom.pmf} for $P(K = k)$ and \ttblue{binom.cdf} for $P(K \textbackslash{}leq k)$.

The parameters are \ttblue{k}, \ttblue{n}, and \ttblue{p}, where in this case $p = M / N = 17 / 331$.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{binom.pmf(k=1, n=60, p=17 / 331), binom.cdf(1, 60, 17 / 331)}
\end{Verbatim}


\end{exercise}

\begin{exercise}
\textbf{Poisson Distribution}


Use \ttblue{poisson.pmf} and \ttblue{poisson.cdf} with parameter $\textbackslash{}mu = n\textbackslash{}pi$. For this case, $\textbackslash{}mu = 60 \textbackslash{}cdot 17/331$.

This gives a compact way to compare individual-event probabilities and cumulative probabilities in the same setting.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{mu = 60 * 17 / 331}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{poisson.pmf(1, mu), poisson.pmf(3, mu)}
\end{Verbatim}

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{poisson.cdf(1, mu)}
\end{Verbatim}


\end{exercise}

\begin{exercise}
\textbf{Normal Distribution}


Use \ttblue{norm.cdf(x, loc, scale)} with \ttblue{x} as the observed value, \ttblue{loc} as the mean, and \ttblue{scale} as the standard deviation of the sampling distribution.

You can pass arguments positionally or by name. Using named arguments is often clearer for review and makes reordering arguments safe.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{scale = 115.26 / math.sqrt(200)}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{norm.cdf(x=1012, loc=1030, scale=scale)}
\end{Verbatim}

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{norm.cdf(1012, 1030, scale), norm.cdf(x=1012, loc=1030, scale=scale), norm.cdf(scale=scale, x=1012, loc=1030)}
\end{Verbatim}


\end{exercise}

\begin{exercise}
\textbf{Student's t Distribution}


First compute the t-statistic from the sample information, then evaluate the cumulative probability with \ttblue{t.cdf(x, df)}.

This links the standardized value to its probability under Student's t distribution with the chosen degrees of freedom.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{t_val = (1004 - 1030) / (73.8 / math.sqrt(10))}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{t_val, t.cdf(x=t_val, df=9)}
\end{Verbatim}


\end{exercise}

\begin{exercise}
\textbf{Chi-Squared Distribution}


Use \ttblue{chi2.ppf(q, df)} to obtain critical values (quantiles) of the $\textbackslash{}chi^2$ distribution.

Here we compute both an upper quantile (95\%) and a lower quantile (5\%), which are then used for variance-interval reasoning.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{chi2.ppf(q=0.95, df=9), chi2.ppf(q=0.05, df=9)}
\end{Verbatim}


\end{exercise}

\begin{exercise}
\textbf{F Distribution}


Use \ttblue{f.ppf} to get the critical value for hypothesis testing, and \ttblue{f.sf} to compute the right-tail probability of that statistic.

This mirrors the chapter logic: compare your test statistic to the critical value, then interpret the tail probability in context.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{f_crit = f.ppf(q=0.95, dfn=25, dfd=23)}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{f_crit}
\end{Verbatim}

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{f.sf(x=f_crit, dfn=25, dfd=23)}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{exercise}
